data.title: Electronics lift industrial output
data.description:
WIDER BENEFITS: Rising AI adoption started lifting some traditional industries, such as machinery and base metals, even as officials expected factory momentum to moderate

The industrial production index rose 22.95 percent year-on-year to 139.44 last month, lifted mainly by the information technology and electronics sectors, the Ministry of Economic Affairs said yesterday.

Last month’s stronger-than-expected performance was driven by robust demand for artificial intelligence (AI) products, which boosted output in the computer, electronics and optical products; electronic components, including semiconductors; and machinery sectors, Department of Statistics Deputy Director-General Chen Yu-fang (陳玉芳) told a news conference in Taipei.

Output of computer, electronics and optical products rose 45.05 percent year-on-year, and that of electronic components increased 30.37 percent, while production of flat-panel displays fell 19.31 percent, down for a second consecutive month, Chen said.

Flat-panel output is expected to recover through the end of this quarter as international brands front-load orders ahead of the holiday shopping season, she said.

In traditional industries, machinery output rose 17.24 percent for a fourth consecutive month of growth, due to demand for chipmaking and pollution-control equipment and components, Chen said.

Base metal output rose 7.43 percent, supported by demand for electronic components and power batteries, as well as a low comparison base a year earlier, she said.

Chemical material output fell 11.66 percent due to weak demand and maintenance at some production lines, while auto parts output declined 1.72 percent amid a sluggish market, she said.

Overall demand in traditional industries has yet to show a clear recovery, given weak global economic conditions and competition from low-priced Chinese products, Chen said.

However, rising adoption of AI applications has begun to support some traditional sectors, including machinery and base metals, she said.

In the first half, the industrial production index rose 19.82 percent year-on-year, while the manufacturing production index increased 21.24 percent, ministry data showed.

This month, the manufacturing production index is projected to rise 22 to 25.5 percent year-on-year, Chen said, adding that production momentum in the manufacturing sector would moderate in the second half of the year due to a higher comparison base.

However, robust AI demand and launches of new consumer electronics such as AI PCs are expected to continue supporting the momentum, with full-year manufacturing output forecast to maintain double-digit growth, she said.

data.title: Power use rises amid AI activity, keeps index ‘red’
data.description:
GREEN POWER SHIFT: Semiconductor and steel demand stayed strong, while renewable purchases made Taipower sales a weaker gauge of factory activity

Taiwan’s industrial electricity consumption rose again last month, reinforcing signs of solid artificial intelligence (AI)-driven economic activity even as sales by state-owned utility Taiwan Power Co (Taipower, 台電) declined, Taiwan Research Institute (TRI, 台灣綜合研究院) said yesterday.

The institute-compiled Electricity Prosperity Index remained in the “red,” indicating “booming” activity, for the 15th consecutive month last month after high-voltage industrial electricity consumption increased 0.58 percent from a year earlier, it said.

The institute uses a five-color system to gauge Taiwan’s power consumption and economic activity, with “red” indicating “booming,” “yellow-red” signaling warming, “green” representing stable growth, “yellow-blue” signifying a sluggish economy and “blue” implying an economic contraction.

The increase came despite a 2.25 percent decline in Taipower’s high-voltage electricity sales, with sales to manufacturers falling 2.56 percent and those to the service sector slipping 0.76 percent, the institute said.

TRI attributed the divergence to growing corporate purchases of renewable energy as companies increase the share of green power in their energy mix.

As a result, Taipower’s electricity sales no longer fully reflect actual industrial power demand, the institute said.

The semiconductor sector remained the largest consumer of electricity, with power usage rising 8 percent from a year earlier, supported by sustained demand for advanced chip manufacturing, advanced packaging technologies and high-performance computing chips, the institute said.

Electricity consumption in the computer, electronics and optical products sector increased 3.46 percent, driven by robust production of AI servers and networking equipment amid continued investment in AI infrastructure, it said.

The steel industry also benefited from AI-related demand, with electricity consumption climbing 10.18 percent as orders for AI servers, power equipment and public infrastructure projects supported production, it said.

By contrast, most traditional industries continued to face headwinds, the institute said.

While electricity consumption in the machinery and equipment sector edged up 0.05 percent, the chemical materials industry recorded a 9.24 percent decline amid persistent global oversupply and expanded production capacity abroad, it said.

Power consumption in the textile industry also fell 11.46 percent as weak end-market demand and intensifying competition from low-priced Chinese products weighed on output, it said.

Rising electricity consumption among semiconductor manufacturers and resilient export orders suggest that AI-related industries would remain the key engine of Taiwan’s economy, the institute said.

It estimated that Taiwan’s economy expanded 11.1 percent last month and projected second-quarter GDP growth of 11.3 percent.

As AI technologies mature and gain wider adoption, they are expected to accelerate supply-chain upgrades, enhance the competitiveness of Taiwan’s semiconductor sector and sustain economic growth, it said.

data.title: Retail, food, beverage sales reach June record amid recovery of auto market
data.description:
Retail and food and beverage sales last month hit a record for June, benefiting from a recovery in the auto market, new store openings, more promotional campaigns and seasonal demand for cold beverages, data released yesterday by the Ministry of Economic Affairs showed.

Retail sales increased 8 percent from a year earlier to NT$421.3 billion (US$13.06 billion) last month, rising for the fifth consecutive month and beating the ministry’s forecast of a 4 to 7 percent increase.

Sales were driven mainly by increased purchases of cars, motorcycles, auto parts and accessories, which saw significant 19.1 percent year-on-year sales growth, the ministry said in a report.

Department stores, big-box stores and convenience stores, e-commerce and mail-order operators, as well as computer and home appliance outlets also contributed to sales growth during the month, rising from 3.2 to 8.9 percent, the report said.

Retail fuel sales increased 11.9 percent due to rising crude oil prices, it said.

Food and beverage sales rose 4.6 percent year-on-year last month to NT$89.2 billion with across-the-board growth at restaurants, beverage outlets and catering service providers, meeting the ministry’s projection of 4.2 to 7.2 percent growth, the report said.

Underpinned by the strong performance last month, retail sales in the second quarter increased 6.1 percent to NT$1.24 trillion, beating the ministry’s estimates of a 4.7 to 5.7 percent increase, and cumulative sales in the first half of the year climbed 4.1 percent to NT$2.46 trillion, a record for the period and also exceeding the ministry’s projection of 3.4 to 3.9 percent growth, it said.

In the second quarter, food and beverage sales rose 4.7 percent to NT$269.3 billion, in line with the ministry’s 4.5 to 5.5 percent prediction, and from January to last month, sales increased 4.2 percent to NT$553.5 billion, also a record for the same period and in line with the ministry’s 4.1 to 4.6 percent forecast, it added.

Sales in the two sectors this month are expected to continue their growth trend, benefiting from the peak summer travel season, increased demand for cold beverages and business opportunities related to sporting events, the ministry said.

Retail sales this month are expected to grow 8.4 to 11.4 percent to between NT$421.3 billion and NT$432.9 billion, while food and beverage sales would increase 4.7 to 7.7 percent to between NT$90.6 billion and NT$93.2 billion, it said.

data.title: JC Tech Secures Exclusive Taiwan Distribution Rights for AI Counter-UAS Radar, Plans Local ODM Production
data.description:
JC Tech, a Taiwanese drone systems integrator, has signed a strategic cooperation agreement with a leading international defense radar company, securing the exclusive distribution rights in Taiwan for its AI-powered Active Electronically Scanned Array (AI AESA) Counter-Unmanned Aircraft Systems (Counter-UAS) radar solutions.

Integrating the “spear” of indigenous strike drone technologies with the “shield” of advanced Counter-UAS radar capabilities, the partnership establishes Taiwan’s first fully integrated low-altitude defense platform, combining offensive operations, defensive protection and systems integration. The agreement also includes an Original Design Manufacturing (ODM) partnership to manufacture Counter-UAS radar systems in Taiwan, combining Taiwan-developed drone technologies with locally manufactured radar systems for global supply, strengthening Taiwan’s position in the global non-red supply chain while meeting growing international demand for low-altitude defense technologies.

Designed for military operations and critical infrastructure protection, the integrated platform combines AI edge computing, modular payload architecture and AI AESA Counter-UAS radar technologies, enabling tactical reconnaissance, intelligence gathering, precision strike missions and industrial inspection. Following system integration, the platform will be capable of detecting drones as small as 35 cm at ranges exceeding 10 km, providing all-weather surveillance for airports, seaports, energy facilities and other critical infrastructure.

The integrated platform also supports multi-target tracking and AI-assisted target recognition, providing operational capabilities covering low-altitude threat detection, target identification, tracking and countermeasure operations.

The collaboration represents a significant milestone for Taiwan’s defense technology sector, positioning JC Tech among the few Taiwanese companies possessing indigenous strike drone technologies, Counter-UAS radar capabilities and complete systems integration expertise. Together with localized radar manufacturing, the partnership further reinforces Taiwan’s role in the rapidly expanding non-red defense supply chain.

JC Tech has invested extensively in indigenous drone research and development, establishing comprehensive in-house capabilities spanning aircraft design, manufacturing and systems integration. Its core technologies are developed entirely outside the Chinese supply chain, making it one of Taiwan’s first drone manufacturers to achieve fully indigenous drone development and production.

In 2025, the company was placed on China’s export control list, highlighting the strategic importance of its proprietary technologies and its contribution to the non-red supply chain. Its technologies have also been validated in demanding operational environments, attracting growing attention from government agencies and defense organizations across Japan, South Korea, the United States and the Middle East.

To accelerate international expansion, JC Tech recently joined forces with Taiwanese optical thin-film specialist ProOptics Corporation and Oplus Technology to establish Oplus Group, integrating expertise in optical modules, power systems, AI edge computing and drone manufacturing to deliver comprehensive low-altitude defense and smart inspection solutions.

In late June, Oplus Group participated in an international investment forum hosted by JD WealthWise Capital in Tokyo, where it held fundraising discussions with more than ten leading Japanese investment institutions and family offices. The group’s indigenous drone technologies and vertically integrated supply chain attracted significant investment interest from international investors, with several Japanese and U.S. corporations and institutional investors expressing interest in potential investments and strategic partnerships.

Through JD WealthWise Capital’s international empowerment platform, Oplus Group has established connections with global investors, strategic partners and cross-border capital market resources, laying a solid foundation for future international expansion and capital market positioning. The Tokyo forum also highlighted the growing international recognition of Taiwan’s indigenous drone technologies and the increasing strategic importance of the non-red supply chain in the rapidly evolving low-altitude defense industry.

Looking ahead, JC Tech will continue integrating drone platforms, AI edge computing, advanced sensing technologies and Counter-UAS radar systems to build a comprehensive low-altitude defense ecosystem supporting military defense, public safety, industrial inspection, smart agriculture, border security and international technology cooperation. Through localized manufacturing and rapid deployment capabilities, the company aims to help global partners establish secure, autonomous and sustainable unmanned systems supply chains while accelerating the deployment of trusted low-altitude defense technologies in international markets.

“This partnership completes the critical ‘spear and shield’ architecture of Taiwan’s low-altitude defense capabilities,” said Robert Cheng, CEO of JC Tech. “By integrating indigenous strike drone technologies with internationally advanced Counter-UAS radar capabilities, we are strengthening Taiwan’s leadership in defense innovation and the non-red supply chain. Through JD WealthWise Capital’s international empowerment platform, we will continue expanding into Japan, the United States, South Korea, the Middle East and Southeast Asia while delivering trusted, autonomous and cost-effective unmanned system solutions.”

The collaboration is expected to further strengthen Taiwan’s position as a key supplier within the global non-red low-altitude defense supply chain.

data.title: Ubiqconn expects rebound in second half of this year
data.description:
Industrial computer maker Ubiqconn Technology Inc (攸泰科技) on Tuesday said it expects business to recover in the second half of this year, on improved shipments of maritime products and growing contributions from its new US subsidiary, E3 Displays LLC.

Revenue in the first half of this year fell 18 percent from a year earlier to NT$941.71 million (US$29.19 million) due to weak demand in the maritime market and delays to several projects, Ubiqconn spokesman Jason Huang (黃堅真) said in Taipei.

Maritime products accounted for the largest share of Ubiqconn’s sales in the first half of this year at 57 percent, he said.

The company has secured five new maritime projects and started small-volume shipments, Huang said.

The new projects are expected to contribute low-single-digit revenue growth this year and remain an important source of growth through 2029, he added.

Integration of E3 Displays, which Ubiqconn acquired in March last year, is progressing as planned, Huang said.

Through the subsidiary — which focuses on the aerospace, medical, government and industrial display markets — Ubiqconn aims to access US government and large corporate tenders to lift its overall profitability, he said.

The company has observed signs of recovery in the US rugged computer market and plans to expand its portfolio of rugged computers, in-vehicle systems and transportation-related products to meet demand, Huang said.

The US and Europe are the company’s two largest markets, accounting for more than 70 percent of its total revenue, he said.

Across Asia, the company is expanding its presence in Japan, Southeast Asia and India, he said.

data.title: CIER lifts GDP growth to 10.35%
data.description:
MILESTONE: Taiwan’s exports could surpass US$900 billion this year, setting a record and representing growth of about 50 percent from last year, CIER’s president said

The Chung-Hua Institution for Economic Research (CIER, 中華經濟研究院) yesterday raised its GDP growth forecast for Taiwan this year to 10.35 percent, the highest among domestic research institutes, citing stronger-than-expected exports in the first half of the year.

Domestic demand is expected to contribute 4.73 percentage points to this year’s growth, while external demand would add 5.62 percentage points, the institute said.

The upgrade highlighted the strength of artificial intelligence (AI)-related demand and robust orders from major US technology companies, CIER president Lien Hsien-ming (連賢明) told a news conference in Taipei.

“If the momentum continues, Taiwan’s exports could surpass US$900 billion this year, setting a new record,” Lien said.

That would represent growth of about 50 percent from last year and serve as a major driver of economic expansion, he said.

Taiwan has become one of the biggest beneficiaries of the global AI investment boom, with its semiconductor industry playing a critical role in supplying advanced chips, servers and other hardware essential to data centers and AI infrastructure, the institute said.

CIER expects economic growth to remain elevated, but gradually slow through the rest of the year, forecasting GDP growth of 14.55 percent in the first quarter, 10.96 percent in the second quarter, 10.63 percent in the third quarter and 6.04 percent in the final three months.

The expected slowdown reflects a high comparison base rather than a significant weakening in underlying demand, Lien said.

The institute also slightly raised its forecast for consumer inflation to 2.02 percent this year, just above the central bank’s 2 percent target.

It is too early to determine whether stronger inflation pressures would prompt the central bank to consider raising interest rates, Lien said, adding that the outlook would depend heavily on energy prices and geopolitical developments.

Inflationary pressures could ease if global risks subside, he said.

Tsai Yu-tai (蔡鈺泰), head of the statistics department at the Directorate-General of Budget, Accounting and Statistics, said some producer-price increases have been passed on to consumers, particularly in memory chips, computers and other electronic products.

However, such items account for 1.6 percent of the consumer price index basket, limiting their overall impact on inflation, he said.

data.title: FTC extends review of Grab-Foodpanda deal
data.description:
LABOR CONCERNS: Delivery workers protested outside the commission’s office urging the regulator to reject the acquisition over concerns it would stifle competition

The Fair Trade Commission (FTC) yesterday extended its review of Grab Holdings Ltd’s proposed acquisition of Delivery Hero SE’s Foodpanda business in Taiwan to Oct. 27, saying more time was needed to assess the potential impact of Uber Technologies Inc’s 13 percent stake in Grab on competition.

In accordance with the Fair Trade Act (公平交易法), the commission extended the review period by 60 working days, moving the deadline from Wednesday next week to Oct. 27, it said in a statement.

The commission said that questions remain over whether Uber’s stake and 3.7 percent voting rights in Grab could affect Uber Eats’ and Grab’s “incentive and ability” to compete after the acquisition.

Therefore, “further analysis” is needed to determine the deal’s impact on competition in Taiwan, it said.

Uber Eats and Foodpanda are Taiwan’s two leading food delivery platforms.

Uber agreed to a US$14.8 billion deal to acquire Delivery Hero, excluding Foodpanda’s Taiwan operations, which are subject to a separate proposed US$600 million sale to Grab Holdings.

Singapore-based Grab submitted a complete merger application to the commission on June 16.

That triggered a 30-working-day review period that would otherwise have expired on Wednesday next week, the commission said.

Meanwhile, several dozen delivery workers and supporters yesterday protested outside the commission’s office in Taipei, urging the regulator to reject the proposed acquisition over concerns it could create a de facto monopoly.

Chanting “Fight monopolies, demand competition,” the protesters held placards, with some standing beside motorcycles they use to make deliveries.

“Platforms can pursue acquisitions, but we cannot allow Taiwan’s market to be left with two companies in name only that, in practice, share common interests and do not genuinely compete,” National Delivery Union chairman Arery Chen (陳昱安) said.

The union does not oppose Grab’s entry into Taiwan, but the extent of Uber’s influence over Grab should be clarified, particularly given that Uber is Grab’s largest shareholder, Chen said.

Delivery driver Leo Hsu said that workers like him would “probably be in a pretty bad situation” if the acquisition went ahead, adding that if Taiwan’s two leading delivery platforms are under Uber’s influence, the resulting lack of competition could allow it to dictate prices and commissions.

data.title: Unemployment ticks up as graduates enter job market
data.description:
Taiwan’s unemployment rate rose to 3.34 percent last month from 3.27 percent in the previous month, as a fresh wave of graduates entered the job market, the Directorate-General of Budget, Accounting and Statistics (DGBAS) said yesterday.

Nevertheless, last month’s figure was the lowest for June in 26 years, while broader labor-market indicators continued to point to resilience amid the nation’s technology-driven economic expansion, the agency said.

The seasonally adjusted rate climbed to 3.33 percent from 3.32 percent, it said.

For the first six months of this year, the unemployment rate averaged 3.31 percent, down 0.02 percentage points from a year earlier and the lowest level for the period in 26 years, the DGBAS said.

Last month, the number of unemployed people rose by 9,000, or 2.26 percent, from the previous month to 402,000, with first-time job seekers accounting for most of the increase at 5,000, the agency’s data showed.

However, compared with a year earlier, the number of unemployed people fell by 2,000, or 0.35 percent, suggesting stable labor-market conditions, DGBAS Census Department Deputy Director Tan Wen-ling (譚文玲) told a news conference in Taipei.

Unemployment is expected to rise this month and next month as more graduates enter the labor market, Tan said.

The number of underemployed workers — those working fewer hours than desired — rose to 121,000, up about 4,000 from a month earlier and 10,000 from a year earlier, but the increase remained within a normal range and did not indicate a weakening trend, she said.

However, young workers continued to face challenges, with the unemployment rate for those aged 15 to 24 being 11.57 percent, reflecting the influx of first-time job seekers during graduation season, the agency said.

The rate was 5.77 percent for those aged 25 to 29 and 3.36 percent for people aged 30 to 34, it said.

Meanwhile, the average duration of unemployment fell to 19.8 weeks from 20.9 weeks a month earlier.

The number of long-term unemployed people — defined as those out of work for more than one year — declined to 46,000, down 3,000 from the previous month and a year earlier, DGBAS data showed.

data.title: Wistron opens first US smart manufacturing facility
data.description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) server maker Wistron Corp (緯創) on Tuesday opened its smart manufacturing facility in Fort Worth, Texas, where one of Nvidia Corp’s most advanced AI systems has entered mass production.

The D1 facility is Wistron’s first manufacturing base in the US. Built at a cost of US$700 million, it spans approximately 30,100m2.

The Fort Worth plant would be the first to mass produce Nvidia’s GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra chips in the US, Wistron chairman Simon Lin (林憲銘) said at the opening ceremony.

It would also be capable of manufacturing Nvidia’s next-generation Vera Rubin chips, he said.

The D1 plant, which has an automation rate of about 60 percent, is only the beginning, Lin said, adding that Wistron is already planning a second facility, D2, which is expected to be twice the size of D1.

As Wistron expands its advanced manufacturing capabilities, it will continue to develop a local supply chain covering everything from chips to complete systems, he said.

The next stage of the AI industry would not be built from a single production base, but through a global presence, Lin said.

In addition, Wistron chose to establish itself “at the heart of technology” and grow together with its partners, he added.

Representative to the US Alexander Yui (俞大?), Nvidia founder and CEO Jensen Huang (黃仁勳) and Forth Worth Economic Development Department Director Jessica Rogers also attended the ceremony.

Wistron acquired the D1 plant in April last year and began mass production this month to meet customer demand, Wistron president and CEO Jeff Lin (林建勳) said.

data.title: Gigabyte’s GIGAIPC forecasts strong growth this year
data.description:
GIGAIPC Co (技宸), Gigabyte Technology Co’s (技嘉) industrial PC subsidiary, expects strong momentum in its hospitality, point-of-sale and factory automation businesses to grow revenue this year by a high-single-digit percentage, general manager Arthur Chuang (莊再華) said yesterday.

The company has secured orders equivalent to about 90 percent of last year’s revenue, Chuang said at a business presentation in Taipei.

Revenue last year rose 33 percent year-on-year to NT$1.86 billion (US$57.45 million), while net profit surged 101 percent to NT$158 million. Earnings per share increased to NT$9.69 from NT$5.94 a year earlier.

The hospitality business accounted for the largest share of the company’s revenue at 30 percent, after GIGAIPC won orders from several of the world’s top 10 restaurant brands, Chuang said.

The projects are primarily focused on the US market, with further expansion slated for Europe and Asia, he said.

GIGAIPC supplies industrial motherboards, embedded systems and edge artificial intelligence (AI) hardware for the projects, while system integrators provide complete solutions, he added.

Boosted by robust demand for AI applications, the factory automation business continued to grow, contributing 14 percent of last year’s revenue, Chuang said.

Semiconductor customers are increasingly adopting agentic AI to adjust equipment parameters and execute corrective actions, he said.

Some customers purchase AI training systems from Gigabyte before deploying them on production lines using GIGAIPC’s edge devices, he added.

AI-related revenue accounted for about 10 percent to 15 percent of the company’s revenue last year and is expected to increase to 15 percent to 20 percent this year, Chuang said.

North America is expected to remain GIGAIPC’s largest market this year, accounting for about 40 percent of total revenue, followed by Taiwan and Europe, he said.

The company posted a compound annual revenue growth rate of 23.4 percent from 2021 to last year, with the momentum expected to continue over the next three to five years on accelerated demand for AI-related applications, he said.

Regarding rising prices for memory, central processing units and other key components, GIGAIPC chairman Etay Lee (李宜泰) said the impact on this year’s orders is limited as the company built up inventories in the fourth quarter of last year.

However, the tight supply for memory is expected to persist through the first half of next year, he said.
